coredata.dc:title: Chitosan Schiff bases-based polyelectrolyte complexes with graphene quantum dots and their prospective biomedical applications
coredata.dc:creator: Hamed, Amira A.
Saad, Gamal R.
Abdelhamid, Ismail A.
Elwahy, Ahmed H.M.
Abdel-Aziz, Marwa M.
Elsabee, Maher Z.
coredata.dc:description: Chitosan (Cs) bis-aldehyde Schiff base derivatives were synthesized by condensation of Cs with three bis-aldehydes namely; butane-1,4-diyl bis(4-formylbenzoate), N,N′-(butane-1,4-diyl)bis(2-(4-formylphenoxy)acetamide) and 4,4′-(butane-1,4-diylbis(oxy))dibenzaldehyde. The prepared Cs derivatives were blended with carboxymethyl chitosan(CMC) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to produce semi-IPNs polyelectrolyte complexes (PECs). and characterized with respect to their molecular structure and physio-chemical properties. The antibacterial activity against H. pylori (and in vitro Inosine 5′-monophosphate dehydrogenase IMPDH inhibitory assay) was evaluated. Additionally, a preliminary in vitro assessment for wound healing was performed against PECs in which wound closure percentages, and rates were investigated indicating an accelerated wound healing compared with untreated cells. The PEC based on Schiff base PEC containing amide linkage showed the highest wound healing ability. A minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) was obtained for the PEC sample containing Cs Schiff base derived from 4,4′-(butane-1, 4-diylbis(oxy))dibenzaldehyde at a dose of 0.98 μg/ml inhibiting H. pylori growth by 100%. Additionally, the selected above-mentioned compound was selected to test its inhibitory activity against the HpIMPDH enzyme in addition to its selectivity towards the hIMPDH2 enzyme and was found to have promising activity against the HpIMPDH enzyme with IC50 value of 0.65 μM, and to be safer and less active against the hIMPDH2 enzyme with IC50 > 10 μM, reflecting its selectivity.

head.ce:title: Chitosan Schiff bases-based polyelectrolyte complexes with graphene quantum dots and their prospective biomedical applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chitosan (Cs) bis-aldehyde Schiff base derivatives were synthesized by condensation of Cs with three bis-aldehydes namely; butane-1,4-diyl bis(4-formylbenzoate), N,N′-(butane-1,4-diyl)bis(2-(4-formylphenoxy)acetamide) and 4,4′-(butane-1,4-diylbis(oxy))dibenzaldehyde. The prepared Cs derivatives were blended with carboxymethyl chitosan(CMC) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to produce semi-IPNs polyelectrolyte complexes (PECs). and characterized with respect to their molecular structure and physio-chemical properties. The antibacterial activity against H. pylori (and in vitro Inosine 5′-monophosphate dehydrogenase IMPDH inhibitory assay) was evaluated. Additionally, a preliminary in vitro assessment for wound healing was performed against PECs in which wound closure percentages, and rates were investigated indicating an accelerated wound healing compared with untreated cells. The PEC based on Schiff base PEC containing amide linkage showed the highest wound healing ability. A minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) was obtained for the PEC sample containing Cs Schiff base derived from 4,4′-(butane-1, 4-diylbis(oxy))dibenzaldehyde at a dose of 0.98 μg/ml inhibiting H. pylori growth by 100%. Additionally, the selected above-mentioned compound was selected to test its inhibitory activity against the HpIMPDH enzyme in addition to its selectivity towards the hIMPDH2 enzyme and was found to have promising activity against the HpIMPDH enzyme with IC50 value of 0.65 μM, and to be safer and less active against the hIMPDH2 enzyme with IC50 > 10 μM, reflecting its selectivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Chitosan Cs, “β-(1–4)-2-amino-2-deoxy-D-glucan (Cs)”, is an amazing natural polymer which is attributed to its robust “biological activity, excellent biocompatibility, and total biodegradability, in addition to its low toxicity [1–3], Cs has emerged as a one-of-a-kind polymer with a wide range of functionalities. The abundant supply of Cs, the variety of modifications, and the presence of reactive amino and hydroxyl groups successfully made Cs a good candidate in a variety of applications, contributing to environmentally sustainable solutions such as water/oil separation [4] and antimicrobial Food Packaging Materials [5]. These qualities led to the intensive biomedical uses of Cs and its derivatives in accelerating wound healing [6], antimicrobial, anti-tumor, and anti-ulcer properties [7,8]. However, Cs has several drawbacks, among which is low solubility under physiological conditions. To address this problem, the derivatization of Cs's hydroxyl and amino groups through chemical modifications can conveniently alter its properties [9,10]. Among the notable modifications in this regard is the reaction of aldehyde and ketones with the Cs amino group which is also known as the Schiff base reaction. Numerous derivatives of Cs Schiff bases have been summarized and published in the literature [11–17] which have many applications in medicine and pharmacology as cancer prevention and antimicrobial agents [12,18,19]. The Cs Schiff bases have a wide range of biological properties, including antiurease activity, and are widely employed for industrial applications [20]. Hemostatic medicines based on Cs are emerging as one of the most promising options for minimizing preoperative and postoperative bleeding [21].

On the other hand, cross-linking is a popular technique for modifying Cs [22,23] in which Cs is reacted with epoxides, anhydrides, or bis-aldehydes. Cross-linking increases desired functionality, such as Cs's acid tolerance, and improves the Cs's absorption properties. It was well known that bis-aldehydes were utilized as precursors for various bis(functionalized) organic compounds that have pharmacological and therapeutic properties [24].

Carboxymethyl chitosan (CMC) a chitosan derivative extensively studied for biomedical applications [25–28]. It is usually in the form of sodium salt with enhanced water solubility over a wide pH range and processes good biocompatibility and these improved properties are attributed to the presence of carboxylate groups on the polymer backbone. CMC was used as an amphoteric polyelectrolyte with anticancer activity and “hemostatic activity by contributing to the decrease of the whole blood clotting time and lowering the plasma recalcification time” [29]. In addition, cross-linked CMC was used to produce hydrogels with swelling and water absorption capacity that support cell attachment and a rapid growth of fibroblasts on this polymer [30]. Therefore, these desirable properties make this derivative suitable for use in pharmaceutical as wound healing, food preservation and tissue engineering [31].

Polyelectrolyte complexes (PECs) are “supramolecular structures created by the interactions of charged macromolecules into a network-shaped structure resulting from physical crosslinking based on the electrostatic attraction of highly hydrophilic polyelectrolyte chains in aqueous solutions [32] which are endowed with intrinsic physicochemical characteristics, such as hydrophilicity, swelling, gelation, mechanical resistance, porosity, biocompatibility, and biodegradability that confer the capacity to be used as antimicrobial agents that may be useful for wound dressings and speeding wound healing” [33–37].

Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), “a zero-dimensional graphene have the same layered structural as graphene, they have more carboxylic groups on the perimeter, making them more compatible with biological systems [38]. Graphene-based nanomaterials” as new generation of materials, have been used in a variety of sectors, such as biological imaging [39], drug delivery [40], and catalysis [41]. In addition, GQDs can also act as the cross-linkers instead of toxic organic molecules, which would improve the biocompatibility of the hydrogels [42–46]. Additionally, GQDs were reported to be acting as antimicrobial materials [47], could activate hemostasis [48] and inhibitors of fibrillation of the protein characteristic for Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease [49] and are the next generation of nanomaterials with great potential as anti-HIV activity [50,51].

In this work, considering benefits of the chitosan Schiff bases, carboxymethyl chitosan and GQDs, these materials were used for the fabrication of a new system for medical application, particularly as antimicrobial agent against H. pylori. The protonation of unreacted amine and imine groups on the backbone of Cs Schiff bases, these derivatives form a cationic polyelectrolyte in an acidic medium, which could associate with negatively charged carboxyl anion on the surface of GQDs and CMC to form physically cross-linked polyelectrolyte complexes (PECs). In contrast to covalently cross-linked gels, ionic complexes retain the main properties of the Cs of their components.

Among bacteria, “Helicobacter pylori is a microaerophilic gram-negative bacterium”. The H. pylori bacterial infection is the leading cause of gastrointestinal ulcers and infections globally. H. pylori infection can raise the chance of stomach cancer, which is the world's second largest cause of cancer mortality. “Because H. pylori resides in the epithelial layer of the mucosa, and the increased drug resistance of H. pylori; therapy with adequate antibiotic concentrations fails” [52,53]. Moreover, the eradication of H. pylori decreases the recurrence of peptic ulcers and leads to long-term peptic ulcer disease remission [54]. It has been reported that the use of Cs as an adjuvant in the H. pylori vaccination decreased H. pylori-induced chronic gastritis [55]. More recently, H. pylori infection has been linked to a variety of disorders outside of the gastrointestinal tract, including cardiovascular disease, diabetes, and hepatobiliary disease (gallbladder cancer), In addition, H. pylori has a unique capacity to survive and colonize the stomach mucosa's severe acidic environment, resulting in long-term infection [56,57].

In this study cross-linked Schiff bases derived from the reaction between Cs and three bis-aldehydes containing different functional groups were synthesized and used to develop semi-interpenetrating network polyelectrolyte complexes with CMC and GQDs. The characteristics of the prepared materials were analyzed by spectroscopic methods, X-ray, and TGA techniques. In addition, these complexes were tested for in vitro wound healing capacity and antibacterial activity which might have the potential to be utilized for the treatment of a variety of ulcers, including those caused by H. pylori.

Chitosan was prepared from chitin extracted from shrimp crust as described previously [58]. Its degree of deacetylation (DD) is 89%, as determined by the titration method [58], The average viscosity molecular weight (
M ¯
v
= 1.9 × 105 Da) was determined from viscosity measurements using 2% acetic acid/0.1 M KCl as a solvent at 25 °C from the following equation [59,60]: ( [ η ] = 1.424 ×
10
− 3
M ¯
v 0.96
)
. The other reagents were acquired and used as purchased from Merck (Germany). Bis-aldehydes namely, butane-1,4-diyl bis(4-formylbenzoate), BAB, N,N′-(butane-1,4-diyl)bis(2-(4-formylphenoxy)acetamide), BAA and 4,4′-(butane-1, 4-diylbis(oxy))dibenzaldehyde, BAE, have been prepared as described previously [61–63].

Carboxymethyl chitosan (CMC) was prepared according to the method described before. The reaction may proceed as O-substitution and/or N-substitution. The O-substitution is described much more widely in the literature [64]. “In this study, CMC was prepared by the reaction of monochloroacetic acid with Cs in the presence of NaOH to activate the nucleophilic properties of the hydroxyl groups. However, under strongly alkaline conditions, the competing N-substitution also may occur [65]. The determined degree of substitution of carboxymethyl groups on the primary hydroxyl and the amino groups was found to be approximately 49.5% and 43.3%, respectively.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared by the pyrolysis of citric acid according to the method reported in [66]. In a brief, 1 g of “citric acid (CA) was taken in a beaker and heated to 200°C. After a few minutes, the liquid CA which was colorless turned to pale yellow and then to orange, confirming the formation of GQDs. The obtained liquid was neutralized to pH 7 by adding it drop by drop into 50 ml of 5 mg ml-1 NaOH solution to obtain an aqueous solution of GQDs.”

As can be seen from the FT-IR (KBR) spectrum of citric acid (CA) in Fig. 1a, CA has the characteristic peaks of CO stretching of carboxylic groups at 1712 cm−1, C-OH stretching at 1150 cm−1, and CH2 rocking at 790 cm−1. For the spectrum of GQDs, the new characteristic peaks appeared at 1412 cm−1 corresponding to the CC skeletal aromatic stretching vibration, arising from the graphitic domain, 1000 cm−1 (C–O–C, stretch), and a broad band at 3000–3100 cm−1 (-OH, stretching of carboxylic group) confirms the basic component of graphene.

On the other hand, the 1H NMR spectrum of the GQDs. Fig. 1b shows an aromatic carbon-rich architecture at 6.2 and 6.8 ppm, The signal characteristic of CH2 and -CH-O appears at 5.7 ppm, and 4.8 ppm, respectively [67].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Cs bis-aldehyde Schiff bases
ce:section.ce:para: Schiff bases of Cs were synthesized as outlined in Scheme 1 . Cs (1.0 g) was dissolved in about 100 ml 2.0% acetic acid and 50 ml ethanol at room temperature. The predetermined amounts of aromatic bis-aldehyde (2.5 wt% with respect to Cs) dissolved in 10 ml ethanol/acetic acid mixture (50/50, v/v) were gradually added to the solution of Cs. The reaction mixtures were left to react at 80 °C with continuous stirring for 12 h. In the end of the reaction time, the product was precipitated using excess ethanol and few drops of NH4OH to adjust the pH to 10.0. To extract the unreacted aldehyde, the precipitate was purified and washed with anhydrous ethanol several times. The precipitate was frozen overnight in distilled water then left to dry at room temperature via solvent exchange with an equal amount of ethanol to water and finally it was washed several times with ethanol, and left to dry at room temperature for further analysis. The obtained Cs Schiff bases are coded here as Cs-BAB, Cs-BAA and Cs-BAE from the reaction of Cs with butane-1,4-diyl bis(4-formylbenzoate), N,N′-(butane-1,4-diyl)bis(2-(4-formylphenoxy)acetamide) and 4,4′-(butane-1,4-diylbis(oxy))dibenzaldehyde derivatives, respectively”.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of polyelectrolyte complexes (PECs) of chitosan Schiff bases/carboxymethyl chitosan/GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Equal weights 0.5 g each of carboxymethyl chitosan (CMC) and Cs Schiff base dissolved in 50 ml 4% acetic acid solution were mixed thoroughly under stirring for several hours, until a slightly turbid mixture solution was obtained. The resulting mixture solution was then poured into NH4OH solution. The formed precipitate was filtered, washed several times with distilled water and then dried in vacuum oven at 80 for 24 h. The prepared samples were coded according to the kind of bis-aldehyde in Cs Schiff base as follows: PE-1, PE-2, and PE-3 which are corresponding to Cs-BAB, Cs-BAA and Cs-BAE.

For the preparation of PECs (Schiff base/CMC/GQDs), 50 ml of 20% GQDs stock solution was added drop wise to Schiff base/CMC in acetic acid with vigorous stirring. The mixture was left overnight till the formation of opaque suspended materials which suggests the formation of polyelectrolyte complexes (PECs), (Scheme 2 ). The precipitate was filtered washed with distilled water and ethanol. The compositions of the PECs are 1:1:2 based on weight ratios of Cs bis-aldehyde Schiff base, CMC, and GQDs, respectively, and coded as follows: PE-G-1, PE-G-2 and PE-G-3 based on the type of used Cs Schiff base.

FT-IR spectra of Cs Schiff bases using KBr pellet were obtained using a Perkin Elmer B25 spectrophotometer in the frequency range 400–4000 cm−1, USA. The 1H NMR spectra were recorded with NMR spectrometer (Bruker AC-400, Germany) using a mixture of trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) and deuterated dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) as solvents and the chemical shifts were expressed in δ ppm relative to TMS as an internal reference. This mixture solvent was found to dissolve the prepared bis-aldehyde Schiff bases.

“X-ray diffractograms of the prepared powder samples were obtained using an X–ray powder diffractometer (a Philips Xpert MPD Pro) with Ni-filter and Cu Kα radiation source at an accelerating voltage/current of 50 kV/40 mA. The scanning rate was 2°/min and the scanning diffraction angle 2θ was between 5° to 80° at 25°C".

“Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted in a (TA Q 2000, USA) Thermal Analyzer with a dynamic heating rate of 10°C min−1 in the temperature range from room temperature to 600°C under a flow of nitrogen (30 ml/min.), the reference material was α-alumina and a platinum sample holder was used. The sample weights for all the experiments were taken in the range of 3-5mg”.

The morphology changes of the surface structure of the prepared Cs Schiff bases were analyzed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), JEOL (JSM-5200, Japan) to investigate morphological changes of the surface structure.”
A JEOL (JEM 2100, Japan) transmission electron microscope (TEM) was used to observe and confirm the formation of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity against Helicobacter pylori using microtiter assay
ce:section.ce:para:
“Antibacterial activity of the tested compounds against Helicobacter pylori ATCC 26695, was determined by a micro-well dilution method. The inoculum of Helicobacter pylori was prepared and the suspensions were adjusted to 106 CFU/ml. The compounds under investigation and the standard drug (Clarithromycin) were prepared in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and subsequent twofold dilutions (125–0.24 μg) were performed in a 96-well plate. Each well of the microplate included 40 μl of the growth medium (Brain Heart Infusion (BHI) plus 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 10 μl of inoculum and 50 μl of the diluted compounds. The Clarithromycin and DMSO were used as positive and negative controls, respectively. The plates were incubated at 37 °C for 3 days, in 5% O2, 10% CO2 and 85% N2 atmosphere. After that, 40 μl of 3- (4, 5-dimethyl-thiazol-2-yl)- 2,5-diphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) at a final concentration of 0.5 mg/ml freshly prepared in water was added to each well and incubated for 30 min. The change to the purple color indicated that the bacteria were biologically active. The inhibition percentage was calculated using the given formula:
% Inhibition = 1 −
Abs
Control − Abs
Sample
Abs
Control
× 100

The concentration of samples (inhibitors) required for 100% of inhibition (MIC 100) was determined from the corresponding dose-response curves. The MIC was taken to the lowest concentration, where no change of color of MTT was determined using an automatic ELISA microplate reader at 620 nm. The MIC values were done in triplicate” [56,68].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro Enzyme inhibition assays HpIMPDH and hIMPDH2 inhibitory activity

ce:section.ce:para: “The highly active compound (PE-G-3) against H. pylori was screened in vitro at different concentrations (10 μM–0.078 μM) in triplicates. The assay was performed in a 200 μl final volume in Black 96-Well Plate (Tarsons Products Pvt. Ltd., Kolkata, India) with a reaction buffer composed of 50 mM Tris–HCl (pH 8.6), 100 mM KCl, and 1 mM dithiothreitol (DTT). Assays were performed using 100 nM HpIMPDH in the presence or absence of test compounds. The assay mixture was incubated for 10 min at 37 °C, and the reaction was initiated by the addition of 250 μM of IMP and 300 μM of NAD+ (substrate buffer). The assay was allowed to proceed at 37 °C for 45 min. Generated NADH was measured by reading the fluorescence (excitation 340 nm, emission 440 nm) at an interval of 1 min using PerkinElmer EnVision Multilabel Reader (Waltham, MA). Amoxicillin, clarithromycin and/or the combination of amoxicillin and clarithromycin in ratio 2:1 was used as a positive control and DMSO as vehicle control. The IC50 value is the concentration of the sample leading to 50% HpIMPDH inhibition was estimated from the graphical plots [56,69]”.

“The hIMPDH2 was purchased from NovoCIB SAS (Lyon, France) and used for in vitro screening of the highly active compound (PE-G-3) against H. pylori and the standard drug at the concentrations (10 μM–0.078 μM) in triplicates. The assay was performed in 96-well plates (Tarsons, 980040) (200 μl final volume) with a reaction buffer composed of 100 mM Tris–HCl (pH 8.6), 100 mM KCl, and 5 mM DTT, 4% vol/vol DMSO plus or minus test compound and 0.15 mU of purified hIMPDH2 enzyme per well (from 1.5 mg/ml stock concentration). The final volume of the enzyme stock solution per well was 2 μl, which was insignificant to cause any change in the final assay buffer composition. The reaction was initiated by the addition of (substrate buffer) 0.2 mM of IMP and 0.2 mM of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD+), and the assay was allowed to proceed at 37 °C for 30 min. The generated NADH was measured by reading the absorbance at 340 nm. At this wavelength, a background of <0.1 optical density was observed with negligible cross-talk between wells. The IC50 value is the concentration of the sample leading to 50% hIMPDH2 inhibition was estimated from the graphical plots [56,70]”.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and in vitro scratch wound healing assay
ce:section.ce:para:
“Human Skin Fibroblast cells line (HSF) was obtained from Nawah Scientific Inc., (Mokatam, Cairo, Egypt). Thereafter, the cultured cells were plated at density 3 × 105/well onto a coated 6-well plate for scratch wound assay and cultured overnight in 10% FBS-DMEM at 37 °C and 5% CO2, as the method described before [71]. After 24 h, horizontal and vertical scratches of similar sizes were introduced into the confluent monolayer. The plate was washed thoroughly with PBS. Control wells were replenished with the fresh medium while test wells were treated with fresh media containing 100 μg/ml of PECs under investigation. Images were taken using an inverted microscope at different time intervals.” The acquired images were analyzed as follows.
A)
Wound Width: “Wound width can be calculated as the average distance between the edges of the scratches; the wound width decreases as cell migration is induced.” The results are displayed as mean ± standard deviation
B)
Migration Rate: “Migration rate is calculated by dividing the time spent in migration according to the formula below:
(3)
R m
=
W i
−
W f
t
where Rm is the rate of cell migration, Wi is the average initial wound width, Wf is the average final wound width, and t is the duration of migration (in hours).”
C)
Wound Area: “Wound area can be calculated by tracing the cell-free area in images using Fiji-ImageJ software. Under normal conditions, the wound area will decrease over time. The results are displayed as mean ± standard deviation.”
D)
Wound Closure%: “The migration rate can be expressed as the percentage of area reduction of wound closure, which increases as cells migrate over time.”
(4)
Wound closure % =
A 0

A t

× 100
Where A0 is the average area of the wound measured immediately after scratching (time zero), and At is the average area of the wound measured (t) hours after the scratch is performed.

“The Human Skin Fibroblast HSF cells were maintained in DMEM media supplemented with100 mg/ml of streptomycin, 100 units/ml of penicillin and 10% of heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum in humidified, 5% (v/v) CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C. Cell viability was assessed by using a colorimetric assay with 2-(4-iodophenyl)-3-(4-nitrophenyl)-5-(2,4-disulfophenyl)-2H-tetrazolium monosodium salt (WST-1) reagent which is based on the cleavage of (WST-1) to formazan by mitochondrial dehydrogenase in viable cells using Abcam kit (ab155902 WST-1 Cell Proliferation Reagent). Aliquots of 50μl cell suspension (3x103 cells) were seeded in 96-well plates and incubated in complete media for 24h. Cells were treated with another aliquot of 50μl media containing PECs under test at serial concentrations (10μg/ml,100 μg/ml). After 48h of exposure, cells were treated with 10μl WST-1 reagent and the absorbance of converted dye was measured after 1h at 450 nm using a BMG LABTECH FLUOstar Omega microplate reader (Allmendgrün, Ortenberg) Cytotoxicity of the compounds was expressed as percentage cell viability (measured as WST-1 reduction) compared to controls.” [72,73].
Data are presented as means ± SD using a sigma plot for statistical computing for at least three independent experiments.
To set an outline for this paper, we begin with a brief overview illustrated in the following figure and will be discussed in Section 3 (Fig. 2 ).

The FTIR spectra for (Cs) and Cs-bis-aldehyde Schiff bases (Cs-BAB, Cs-BAA, and Cs-BAE) are compared as shown in Fig. 3 . The FTIR spectrum of Cs displayed bands at 3000–3500 cm−1 (due to the overlapped stretching vibrations of the OH, NH, NH2 groups), CH stretching was assigned to a band at 2931 cm−1, whereas CO stretching of the acetamide group, CN deformation of the amino group, and -C-O-C- stretching of the glucopyranoside ring were assigned to bands at 1654, 1426, and 1160 cm−1, respectively [17].

Compared with Cs, FTIR spectra of Cs-bis-aldehyde Schiff base derivatives displayed bands in the range of 1647–1688 cm−1 which were assigned to the imine CN group substantiating the reaction between Cs and the bis-aldehyde derivatives. The bands between 2927 and 2895 cm−1 are due to symmetrical and asymmetric CH stretching while the band around 1056 cm−1 in all Schiff bases derivatives is attributed to the aromatic in-plane CH bending [17]. The prominent absorption bands at 1719 and 1706 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of CO in ester and amide moieties in the Cs Schiff base derivatives, respectively [62]. Additionally, the incorporation of bis-aldehyde cross-linker into Cs was confirmed by the characteristic band of aromatic CC around 1500 cm−1 [74].

Comparing the spectra of PECs with their individual components (Cs bis-aldehyde Schiff base derivatives, CMC and GQDs), the peaks around 1600–1750 cm−1 characteristic of carbonyl group present in the Cs derivatives and GQDs that might be related to the formation of ionic crosslinking structure between the protonated unreacted NH2 and NHCH2 groups of Cs derivatives and anionic carboxylate groups of GQDs. However, the presence of the band around at 1633 characteristic of the carbonyl group substantiates the electrostatic ionic interactions between the GQDs and Cs derivatives.

Additionally, the spectra of PECs showed absorption bands in the regions of 1571–1539 cm−1 and 1375–1395 cm−1, which can be assigned to symmetrical and asymmetric vibrations of COO−, respectively, and bands at 1683–1696 cm−1 of the imine groups. This is indicative of the formation of complex formation and incorporation of the Cs derivatives and GQDs in PECs [66,75] (Fig. 4 ).

The 1H NMR spectra of Cs bis-aldehyde Schiff bases were displayed in Fig. 5 . All the spectra exhibited the characteristic 1H NMR pattern of Cs, i.e., the multiplet at 3.3–4.4 ppm due to H3, H4, H5, H6, H6’and two singlets at 3.0 and 1.9 ppm due to the H2 proton and N-acetyl protons of pyranose ring, respectively. Additionally, typical signals were observed in the region 7.0–8.3 ppm correspond to the aromatic ring protons of the Schiff base derivatives. The signal that appeared at 9.0 ppm was assigned to the proton of imine groups. In the spectrum of Cs-BAB derivative, the signal at 4.34 ppm was assigned to the protons of –CH2 next to the ester group of the BAB cross-linker (s, 4H, 2 COOCH2). Additionally, the Cs-BAA spectrum shows signals at 2.8 and 4.58 ppm corresponding to 4H of CH2N and OCH2, respectively. Finally, a signal at 4.09 ppm corresponds to 4H of CH2–O in Cs-BAE spectrum [61–63]. Therefore, it can be deduced that the bis-aldehydes were incorporated onto Cs.

The 1H NMR spectrum of the PECs and CMC (Fig. 6 ) show signals in the range of 2.36–2.98 ppm that confirmed the presence of different types of benzylic protons. The chemical shift at 4.8 ppm is due to the protons of CH2-COO of CMC [76]. The signals appearing in the range of 3.16–3.85 ppm are assigned to –O–CH- groups of protons. On the other hand, NH signals due to the unreacted amino groups in Cs skeleton were detected at 8.3 ppm in PE-G-3 spectra showed a slight broadening, confirming their participation in ionic interactions. The aromatic ring protons of bis-aldehyde derivatives in the PECs showed a slight broadening signal compared to neat Cs bis-aldehyde Schiff base derivatives suggesting the interaction between aromatic rings of base aldehyde moiety and π bonds of the graphenic rings via π-π stacking.

Fig. 7 shows the XRD patterns of Cs and Cs bis-aldehyde Schiff bases. The pattern of Cs showed two characteristic peaks at 20.1o and 10.3o. For Cs Schiff bases, the peak at 2θ =10.3o disappears, and most importantly, the characteristic peak at 2θ = 20.1o becomes wider and less intense than that of Cs, indicating that the Schiff bases exhibited more amorphous character than Cs. The less crystalline structure of Cs Schiff bases might be attributed to the disruption of the ordered structure of Cs by the incorporation of bis-aldehyde cross-linkers.

On the other hand, the change observed in the characteristic diffraction peaks of Cs Schiff bases in the PECs (Fig. 8 ) might be ascribed to the complex formation between Cs Schiff base-CMC and GQDs, through –NH3 + –COO− binding sites, which had efficiently reduced the hydrogen bonding and had destroyed the crystalline structure of CMC and Cs Schiff base derivatives. The diffraction peaks centered at 2θ =24.2 ° can be attributed to the (002) carbon-to‑carbon spacing crystalline graphite-like structure of GQDs [77]. Therefore, the broad nature and low intensities of the observed peaks of the Schiff bases and CMC components in the polyelectrolyte complex suggested the highly amorphous nature of the prepared polyelectrolyte complexes.

TEM images are represented in Fig. 10. The neat GQDs have a spherical shape with size range between 22 nm and 39 nm, while GQDs in the polyelectrolyte complexes possess polyhedron shaped with unambiguous connecting borders with spherical range of 45–86 nm, indicating the involvement of GQDs into the ionic crosslink-like structure between the Cs Schiff bases and CMC via the molecular interaction between the negative charges present on the surface of GQDs, carboxylate group in CMC and the protonated imine of the Schiff base and unreacted amino groups of Cs. Another reason for the greater size of GQDs in the Schiff base of Cs/CMC complex may be related to the aggregates of GQDs in this matrix.

TG/DTG curves of Cs Schiff base/CMC derivatives PE-1, PE-2, and PE-3, and their complexes with GQDs (PE-G-1, PE-G-2, and PE-G-3 PECs are given in Figs. S1a and S1b (Supplementary information). The TGA curve of CMC Schiff base derivatives without GQDs showed two main distinct stages of mass loss. The broad mass loss at temperature between below 180 °C with an initial mass loss about 8–13%, dependent of type of bis-aldehyde, was assigned to the removal of adsorbed and bound water, which merged together, from their network structure. The second stage of mass loss occurring between 200 and 400 °C, was associated with the decomposition of Cs, decarboxylation of CMC base polymer and the imine cross-linkers moieties attached to Cs [11,17]. From TGA curves of PECs with GQDs, it was obviously that the thermograms revealed several stages of mass losses. The mass loss due to free adsorbed water occurred at lower temperature (below 100 °C) followed by bound water below 180 °C with total mass loss about 15, 19, and 22% corresponding to PE-G-2, PE-G-1, and PE-G-3, respectively. However, the mass loss bound water started at higher temperature compared to PECs without GQDs was mainly owing to the formation of a higher crosslink density in the PECs as the results of incorporation of GQDs. The small decomposition stage, which took place in temperature range 185-265 °C with mass loss about 12, 10.5 and 13% corresponding to PE-G-1, PE-G-2, and PE-G-3, respectively is probably due to the decomposition of low molecular organic compounds outflowing from pyrolysis of citric acid during the preparation of GQDs. The main decomposition stage which occurred at higher temperature (262 to 500 °C with mass loss 31, 36 and 35.2% mass loss for PE-G-1, PE-G-2, and PE-G-3, respectively) compared with neat PECs (182-445 °C with mass loss about 39,52 and 27% corresponding to PE-1, PE-2, and PE-3, respectively), may be attributed to the decomposition of Cs Schiff base derivatives, CMC and the core fraction of GQDS [78–80]. Based on the onset decomposition temperature (To) and the maximum decomposition temperature (Tmax), there is a slight improvement in thermal stability with the addition of GQDs to the film, which can possibly be attributed to the increase of molecular interactions and formation of ionic bonds between the polymeric chains of CMC, Cs Schiff bases and GQDs [81]. The thermal stability was found to be increased in the following order: PE-G-2 > PE-G-1 > PE-G-3. Further, the carbonaceous residue of the PECs was found to be higher than that of Cs derivatives in the absence of GQDs. A residual weight of about 33, 31, 28, 37, 38, and 330% were observed for PE-1, PE-2, PE-3, PE-G-1, PE-G-2, and PE-G-3, respectively. These results support the formation of the somewhat ionic cross-linked structure of Cs derivatives via reaction with GQDs [79].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity against Helicobacter pylori using microtiter assay
ce:section.ce:para: In this study the anti-H. pylori experiment was carried out using the tetrazolium salt (MTT) color reduction%, “which is inversely proportional to H. pylori cell viability”. The results are displayed in Fig. 11 for the prepared Cs derivatives (PE-1, PE-2, PE-3, PE-G-1, PE-G-2, and PE-G-3) in addition to Clarithromycin as the standard drug against H. pylori. The results imply that PE-G-3 PEC was more potent against H. pylori compared to the other Cs derivatives. PE-G-3, in particular, at a concentration of 0.98 μg/ml inhibits H. pylori growth by 100% compared to 29.82 and 81.94% inhibition by PE-G-2, and PE-G-1, respectively. The minimal inhibitory concentration MIC required to kill 100% of the H. pylori of Cs Schiff base derivatives was also shown in Table 1 . The data reported in Table 1 show that PE-G-3 exceeds all Cs derivatives under investigation, and Clarithromycin in its anti-H. pylori activities. PE-G-3 appears to be a potential anti-H. pylori candidate based on these findings. Additionally, PE-G-3 was found to exhibit a higher anti H. pylori activity compared to previously published studies as can be seen from Table 1S.

The antimicrobial activity of the PECs could be attributed to Cs derivatives which possesses a polycationic characteristic that electrostatically interacts with the negatively charged membranes of the microbes' leading to leakage of intracellular constituents, Cs binds with bacterial DNA, thus inhibiting mRNA and consequently protein synthesis, it binds with divalent ions like Ca2+and Mg2+present in the microorganism cell wall which prevents the production of toxins and inhibits bacterial growth. Cs stacks on the microbial cell wall forming a layer on its surface which blocks nutrients and oxygen from entering the cell [82,83].

Moreover, it was found that graphene quantum dots (GQDs) could produce reactive oxygen species ROS disrupt bacterial cell membrane integrity, and ultimately kill the bacteria [84]. “GQDs disperse Staphylococcal aureus biofilms by competitive assembly with amyloid peptides signifying a potential low therapeutic dosing and high in vivo biocompatibility of GQDs [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enzyme inhibition assays HpIMPDH and hIMPDH2 inhibitory activity of the selected compound PE-G-3 and clarithromycin
ce:section.ce:para: “Inosine-5′-monophosphate dehydrogenase (IMPDH) has been studied as a potential target to treat H. pylori infection.” Our work aims to overcome the drawback of existing inhibitors by introducing new Cs derivatives for targeting bacterial IMPDH. Inosine-5′-monophosphate dehydrogenase (IMPDH) has been shown to be a viable therapeutic target for a variety of disorders Some pathogens, such as Cryptosporidium parvum (C. parvum) has been identified as possible target for IMPDH Inhibition of IMPDH can prevent the guanine nucleotide pool from expanding, which is required for microbial growth. The bacterial IMPDH enzyme, which is involved in the nucleotide biosynthesis pathway, has lately attracted a lot of interest as a potential target for treating multidrug-resistant diseases [85]. Based on the in vitro assay of PE-G-3 PEC against H. pylori, and the standard drug clarithromycin was tested for in vitro HpIMPDH inhibition. For the IC50 measurement, the enzyme inhibition assay was determined by measuring the generation of NADH at compound concentrations ranging from 10 μg/ml to 78 ng/ml. In vitro inhibition of human IMPDH2 (hIMPDH2) was also investigated to demonstrate the selectivity of the tested compounds. The concentration response curves were created by plotting an average of fluorescence responses at the plateau of the inhibitor's fluorescence–time curves against their corresponding concentrations. The activity data (% inhibition and IC50) of PE-G-3 and the clarithromycin are given in Table 2 which illustrated that compounds PE-G-3 have promising activity against the HpIMPDH enzyme with IC50 values of 0.65 μM, whereas the IC50 values of the reference drug clarithromycin were 0.51 μM. Moreover, PE-G-3 was less active and safer against hIMPDH2 with IC50 > 10 μM, compared with the reference drug (IC50: 3.1 μM), which makes sure that its selectivity is towards HpIMPDH and reverse to that of clarithromycin.

In a prior investigation, it was discovered that exposure to GQDs derivatives could selectively alter endogenous antioxidant enzyme activity, perhaps by competitive suppression of electron transport [86]. GQDs-based nanomaterials' physicochemical qualities as HIV reverse transcriptase (RT) inhibitors, as has been described in the literature with comparable polyanionic chemicals, the polycarboxylated GQDs could reduce RT activity by inhibiting virus binding to cells [50,51] (Fig. 12 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro scratch wound healing assay
ce:section.ce:para: Wounds take a long time to heal, especially full-thickness ones, and both acute and chronic wounds have always been a serious clinical issue. [87]. As a result, a wound dressing that serves as a barrier and ideally fits the demands of quick wound closure, wound healing, and scar reduction is critical. Wound dressings made of rubber, foam, and electro spun nanofiber have all been created so far [88].

Wound healing is a dynamic and complex process that includes inflammation, proliferation, and remodeling, with fibroblast migration playing an important role in wound healing [89]. It was reported that Cs and its derivatives can be used to fabricate wound dressings with anticipated healing [90].

The major goal and first stage in wound healing are to avoid infection by using bactericidal and broad-spectrum antibiotics, to be biodegradable, biocompatible, and to have no negative impacts on the surrounding in general, such as those employed synthesized Cs PECs derivatives in our research study about the healing rate through avoiding infections, which are the leading cause of wound or ulcer healing delays [91]. For instance, after infection with H. pylori, human gastric epithelial cells demonstrated enhanced cellular motility. H. pylori infections have been shown to affect epithelial cell adhesion and contribute considerably to stomach mucosal injury [92,93]. Additionally, Most duodenal ulcers do not heal following H. pylori eradication will recover after a few weeks [94].

Here, we evaluated the effect of the tested PECs on the wound healing process via in vitro scratch wound assay following a procedure described in the literature [71] in which wound width, area, migration rate, and wound closure % were assessed and the results are shown in Fig. 13 . The preliminary results suggested that the investigated samples facilitate wound healing by increasing the extent of wound closure. In the course of 24 h exposure to the tested three samples, cells moved towards the opening to close the scratch wound by about 53.6, 60.98, and 44.4% for PE-G-1, PE-G-2 and PE-G-3, respectively, indicating a significantly accelerated wound healing process compared to the control. As shown in Fig. 14 , as a representative example, the images of a confluent monolayer of HSF of control untreated cells and the PEC; namely PE-G-2 treated cells that were “wounded” by scratching during different time intervals (immediately, 24, 48, and 72 h). The wound was completely healed after 72 h using PE-G-2, indicating that the greatest effect was found for the PEC with aromatic bis-aldehyde bearing amide groups. This could be attributed to the fact that the amide group is known as the core structural unit in proteins and frequently occurs in a wide variety of biologically active compounds [95]. In vitro assays showed an absence of toxicity, ability to promote migration of human dermal fibroblasts treated with any of Cs Schiff base formulations. These preliminary results suggested that the PECs facilitate healing by increasing the rate and extent of wound closure.

The area of the cell-free gap was assessed upon treatment with 100 μg/ml PE-G-1, PE-G-2, and PE-G-3 at 0, 24, 48 and 72 h of post-treatment. Surprisingly, HSF cells treated with the PECs proliferate and shrink the cell-free gap rapidly than control cells (Fig. 14). The percentage open wound area for the control, and PE-G-2 after 48 h treatment was 78%, and 95%, respectively, while, the average wound width decreased from 1.7 in. after 24 h to 0.4 in. after 48 h., the average wound area decreased from 1,452,774 mm2 on day 1 to 539490mm2 on day 2 for PE-G-2 treated cells.

Migration rates which are key aspects of the wound healing process during the tissue regeneration stage [96] were depicted for partially crosslinked Cs Schiff bases treated cells and untreated cells in Fig. 13 after 48 h migration rates. The values were found to be 0.026, 0.027 and 0.013 in./h corresponding to PE-G-1, PE-G-2, and PE-G-3, respectively.

The synthesized Cs derivatives' cellular toxicity is one of the most important features to consider when evaluating their suitability for use in biomedical applications. Viability assay of the Human Skin Fibroblast cells (HSF) treated with the synthesized Cs Schiff base derivatives PECs (PE-G-1, PE-G-2, and PE-G-3) were monitored using WST-1 assay. The cell viability at a concentration of 100 μg/ml treated cells demonstrated approximately 83.9, 85.9, and 64.9% corresponding to (PE-G-1, PE-G-2, and PE-G-3) compared to 10 μg/ml treated cells which showed 98.7, 99.9, and 97.34%, suggesting that the cytotoxicity was affected by the type of Cs Schiff base. Moreover, in vitro antibacterial activity assessment of PE-G-3 against H. pylori at concentration 15.63 μg/ml indicated complete eradication of H.pylori. Previous research found that Cs and the majority of Schiff bases were not toxic to cells [97].Furthermore, GQDs have previously been proven to exhibit low toxicity on mammalian cells at doses up to 640 g/ml [49]. In conclusion, the cytotoxicity investigations back up the produced PECs safety (Fig. 2S).

Cross-linked Cs Schiff bases were synthesized from the aromatic bis-aldehyde linkers having different spacer functionality, and mixed with CMC and GQDs their structures were characterized by FT-IR, 1H NMR, XRD, SEM, TEM and TGA. The antibacterial performance of the prepared PECs was assessed against H. pylori. The results revealed that the antibacterial dependent on the type of the used Cs Schiff base. It has been also shown that PE-G-3 complex exhibited the most potent activity against H. pylori compared to PE-G-1, PE-G-2 complexes, and the standard drug clarithromycin. The most active compound PE-G-3 with MIC 0.98 μg/ml was selected to test its inhibitory activity against the HpIMPDH enzyme in addition to its selectivity towards the hIMPDH2 enzyme. The results indicated that the PE-G-3 have promising activity against the HpIMPDH enzyme with IC50 value of 0.65 μM, and to be less active and safer against the hIMPDH2 with IC50 > 10 μM, which reflected its selectivity. Furthermore, Preliminary results of in vitro scratch wound healing assay suggested that the investigated PECs facilitate the wound healing process compared with untreated cells, the PEC based on Schiff base PEC containing amide linkage (PE-G-2) showed the highest wound healing ability. Furthermore, all the prepared PECs were found to be non-cytotoxic. Thus, our results suggest that the prepared materials are able to inhibit H. pylori and enhance wound healing.
The authors confirm contribution to the paper. All authors reviewed the results and approved the final version of the manuscript.
